title: IMGUI Container Composition
use_for: designing container-first or surface-first IMGUI interfaces
structuring dashboards, workspaces, sectioned screens, and multi-panel surfaces
choosing and defining composition around containers, sections, strips, grids, and regions instead of window shells
do_not_use_for: host registry and multi-window lifetime policy
single-window shell composition with header or sidebar as the primary abstraction
rules: treat Surface and Container as first-class composition units alongside Window and Widget
use container-first composition when the UI reads as one coordinated surface rather than a set of windows
treat Window as a specialization of container composition, not the default for every screen
let imgui-layout-composition own the shared slot grammar and geometry mechanics after the container-first path has been selected
structure dashboards and workspaces through explicit sections, regions, strips, and panels
keep section and region ownership explicit across IMGUI phases
procedure: Decide whether the UI is surface-first, container-first, or window-first.
Define the top-level surface and its major section containers.
Assign stable regions for metrics, logs, timelines, controls, and detail areas as needed.
Resolve section composition before discussing window-shell specializations.
Verify that the screen still reads as one coherent surface rather than accidental windows.

primary_when: the request is specifically about dashboards, workspaces, surfaces, or container-first IMGUI composition
the request is about sectioned interfaces where window-shell semantics are secondary or absent
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about host-level window lifetime
the request is specifically about reusable base window shells with header, sidebar, and content host

solves: how to design IMGUI interfaces that are organized as one surface instead of a set of windows
how to keep dashboards and multi-section tools structurally coherent
how to separate container-first design choice from shared layout grammar and window-shell composition

core_principles: A coordinated UI surface may be the primary unit, with windows only as optional specializations.
Containers, sections, strips, and regions should express the structure of dashboards and workspaces directly.
Surface-first composition should not be forced through window semantics when the UI does not read as windows.
This skill owns the design-path choice and semantic model of surfaces and containers, not the generic slot grammar.
Region ownership should stay explicit enough to review and evolve.
The same layout grammar can support both container-first and window-first paths.
constraints: Surface-first composition must remain stable across IMGUI phases.
Sections and regions must have explicit ownership and geometry rules.
Container-first screens must not be back-modeled into fake windows unless the interaction model truly needs them.
decision_rules: Use this skill when the UI is perceived as one surface with coordinated sections rather than multiple windows.
After choosing the container-first path, let imgui-layout-composition define slots, nested regions, and manual geometry rules.
Let imgui-modular-windowing own reusable shell composition when the design truly centers on a base window contract.
Let imgui-windowing own registry, focus, restore, and lifetime across multiple windows.
Use explicit containers and regions for dashboards, timelines, logs, metric strips, and workspace panels.
Attach imgui-adaptive-layout when the surface must adapt across resolutions without changing its section map.

preferred_patterns: top-level surface with named section containers
region-based composition for dashboards and workspaces
panel grids, strips, and timelines as explicit container regions
layout grammar delegated to shared composition rules after the container path is chosen
window shells used only where shell semantics are actually needed
manual Rect geometry derived from a retained section map
avoid_patterns: forcing every major region into a fake window shell
treating dashboards as just tiled windows without shared surface ownership
region structure implied only by draw order
container hierarchies that hide section ownership

recipes.name: Compose a diagnostics dashboard as one IMGUI surface
recipes.when: Use when the UI is a coordinated workspace of metrics, logs, and timelines rather than a set of windows.
steps: Define the top-level surface and section boundaries.
Assign fixed or adaptive regions for worker state, timelines, analysis, and logs.
Represent each region as a named container with explicit ownership.
Attach nested layout regions only where they clarify section composition.
Keep the overall surface coherent instead of rephrasing each region as a separate window shell.

pitfalls: Forcing container-first screens into window metaphors they do not need.
Treating every section header as evidence that the UI should be a window shell.
Losing shared surface ownership by modeling all regions as unrelated panels.
Letting region composition drift into draw-order-only layout.
review_checklist: Does the UI read as one coordinated surface or workspace?
Is this skill being used to choose a container-first path rather than to replace shared layout grammar?
Are containers and sections treated as first-class composition units?
Has window-shell composition been reserved for places where shell semantics are real?
Are region boundaries and ownership explicit?
Can the section map survive without inventing fake windows?

references.title: Local Reference: IMGUI uGUI-Inspired Composition
references.interpretation: Use this first for the broader domain stance that container and surface composition can be primary even when modular window shells remain available as a specialization.
references.why: Use as the local reference that connects the window-based and container-based design paths.

references.title: Local Reference: IMGUI Composition
references.interpretation: Use this to align surface and container regions with the domain's existing rules for host-owned composition and manual geometry.
references.why: Use to keep container-first composition consistent with the rest of the domain.
